title: Death of Germanicus
description: The young Roman general Germanicus lies on his deathbed, asking his friends to avenge his poisoning. The painting embodies Poussin's rational, measured classicism and established his reputation in Rome.

title: Massacre of the Innocents
description: A scene of violent drama and emotional intensity depicting Herod's biblical infanticide. Rubens uses dynamic composition, vivid color, and muscular forms to heighten the horror.

title: A Pilgrimage to Cythera
description: Aristocratic couples prepare to depart from the island of love. The painting defines the fête galante genre with its dreamy palette and elegant melancholy.

title: Oath of the Horatii
description: Three Roman brothers swear an oath to fight for their city, their rigid geometric poses embodying civic duty and self-sacrifice. A defining image of Neoclassical painting.

title: The Death of General Wolfe
description: A modern historical event depicted in heroic classical style. West broke precedent by dressing his figures in contemporary clothing rather than ancient togas.

title: Parnassus
description: Parnassus is a seminal Neoclassical fresco. Not like before: contours are sharp and the figures look like sculpture, as Mengs copied classical work such as the Roman sculpture Apollo Belvedere.

title: Grande Odalisque
description: An idealized reclining nude with anatomically elongated spine. Ingres sacrificed anatomical accuracy for sinuous elegance, bridging Neoclassicism with Romantic sensuality.

title: The Third of May 1808
description: Spanish civilians face a firing squad during Napoleon's occupation. The stark lantern light, anonymous executioners, and the central figure's pose create an icon of political protest.

title: The Raft of the Medusa
description: Depicts the aftermath of a real shipwreck caused by government incompetence. Survivors on a makeshift raft signal desperately to a distant ship in this monumental canvas.

title: Liberty Leading the People
description: An allegorical figure of Liberty strides across the barricades of the July Revolution, tricolor flag in hand. The painting fuses real political event with mythic heroism.

title: Wanderer Above the Sea of Fog
description: A solitary figure stands atop a rocky precipice, gazing over a vast, fog-filled landscape. The painting embodies the Romantic notion of the sublime.

title: Rain, Steam and Speed – The Great Western Railway
description: A locomotive charges through atmospheric haze across Maidenhead Bridge. Turner dissolves form into shimmering light and color, anticipating Impressionism.

title: A Burial at Ornans
description: A funeral of an ordinary villager rendered on the monumental scale traditionally reserved for history painting. Courbet democratized subject matter.

title: The Painter's Studio
description: Courbet at his easel surrounded by figures from all walks of life — an allegory of his artistic worldview, combining personal manifesto with social panorama.

title: Woman with a Parrot
description: A sensuous nude reclines with abundant hair and a colorful parrot. Courbet challenges academic nude conventions with unidealized naturalism.

title: Luncheon on the Grass
description: A nude woman sits casually with two clothed men in a forest setting. The painting scandalised contemporary viewers and helped bridge Realism toward Impressionism.

title: Impression, Sunrise
description: A hazy view of Le Havre harbor at dawn. The painting's title inadvertently gave Impressionism its name when a critic used it mockingly.

title: Water Lilies
description: Part of Monet's famous series painted at his garden in Giverny. Reflections, floating plants, and shimmering light merge into near-abstraction.

title: A Sunday on La Grande Jatte
description: Parisians relax by the Seine on a sunny afternoon. Each figure is built from thousands of tiny color dots, creating a mosaic-like shimmer when viewed from a distance.

title: The Port of Saint-Tropez
description: A vibrant harbor scene rendered in bold, mosaic-like brushstrokes. Signac's version of Pointillism uses larger, more confident dabs of color than Seurat's original technique.

title: Mont Sainte-Victoire
description: Part of a series exploring the Provençal mountain through shifting planes of color. Cézanne deconstructs landscape into geometric patches, foreshadowing Cubism.

title: The Basket of Apples
description: Cézanne introduced multiple perspectives and a new sense of flatness in this still life. Rather than optical precision, he focused on structural placement and essential geometric form, allowing the viewer's eye to engage dynamically with shifted planes of the table and tilted basket.

title: The Starry Night
description: Swirling night sky over a quiet village, painted from the window of Van Gogh's asylum room. Thick impasto and spiraling forms convey intense inner emotion.

title: Where Do We Come From? What Are We? Where Are We Going?
description: A monumental painting depicting the cycle of life in a tropical setting. Gauguin considered it his masterpiece and philosophical testament.

title: The Scream
description: An agonised figure clutches its face against a blood-red sky. One of the most iconic images in modern art, expressing existential anxiety and modern alienation.
medium: Oil, tempera and pastel on cardboard

title: Vision After the Sermon
description: Breton women witness Jacob wrestling the angel after a sermon. Bold outlines and a flat red ground create a radical departure from naturalistic painting.

title: The Apparition
description: Moreau depicts the biblical Salome witnessing a mystical vision of the severed head of John the Baptist. The painting is a hallmark of Symbolism, featuring an ornate, dreamlike atmosphere that rejects naturalism in favor of an internal, spiritual reality.

title: The Eye Like a Strange Balloon Mounts Toward Infinity
description: A bizarre and lithographic image from Redon's 'noirs' period. It presents a giant eye as a balloon ascending toward the heavens, evoking a surreal and internal dream world that was 'surreal before surrealism.'

title: Death and Life
description: Klimt creates a stark allegorical contrast between the grim figure of Death and the vibrant, overlapping flow of human life. The painting uses decorative patterns and non-naturalistic space to represent the eternal cycle of existence and the inner states of hope and mortality.

title: Woman with a Hat
description: A landmark of Fauvism, this portrait of Matisse's wife exemplifies the 'liberation of color' from its descriptive function. By using wild, non-naturalistic hues for skin and clothing, Matisse prioritized expressive impact and the internal logic of the painting over a literal representation of reality.

title: Dance
description: Five nude figures join hands in a circle dance against a blue-green landscape. This version, Dance (I), was a preparatory study for the larger composition now in the Hermitage, and it captures Matisse's pursuit of pure, joyful color and simplified form.

title: Self-Portrait with Physalis
description: Schiele's angular, distorted self-portrait conveys psychological intensity and physical vulnerability. The contorted pose and stark lines are hallmarks of Viennese Expressionism.

title: Street, Berlin
description: Fashionable pedestrians on a Berlin street rendered in jagged forms and acid colors. The claustrophobic composition captures the nervous energy of modern urban life.

title: Composition VII
description: A large-scale abstract painting combining swirling forms, vibrant colors, and dynamic spatial tension. Kandinsky considered it the pinnacle of his pre-war abstract work.

title: Les Demoiselles d'Avignon
description: Five nude women rendered in fractured, angular forms inspired by Iberian and African art. The painting is considered a proto-Cubist landmark that ruptured classical perspective.

title: Guernica
description: Picasso's monumental anti-war painting depicts the horrors of the bombing of Guernica. Fragmented forms, a screaming horse, and a dismembered soldier compose this Cubist protest.

title: Bird in Space
description: A slender, polished bronze form that distills the idea of flight into pure ascending movement. Brancusi's radical simplification became a touchstone of modern sculpture.

title: Dynamism of a Dog on a Leash
description: A dachshund and its owner's feet are shown in rapid motion, legs multiplied across the canvas. Balla captures movement through sequential phases inspired by chronophotography.

title: Dynamism of a Car
description: A roaring car vibrating with speed, Russolo captures the Futurist obsession with machinery and modern velocity. The arrow-like forms and fragmented space represent the car's movement through the atmosphere, embodying the manifesto's claim that a racing car is more beautiful than the Nike of Samothrace.

title: Dynamism of a Soccer Player
description: In this landmark Futurist painting, Boccioni deconstructs the human figure into swirling fields of color and motion. It illustrates 'universal dynamism' — the idea that objects and their environment are in a state of constant flux and interpenetration, merging together in time and space.

title: Unique Forms of Continuity in Space
description: A striding figure appears to melt and flow as it moves forward. The sculpture is the supreme expression of Futurist concepts of speed, force, and material transformation.

title: Fountain
description: A standard porcelain urinal signed 'R. Mutt' and submitted to an exhibition. The most famous readymade, it challenged the institutional definition of art itself.

title: Bicycle Wheel
description: A bicycle fork and wheel inverted and mounted on a kitchen stool. Considered the first readymade, it questions craftsmanship, originality, and the nature of sculpture.
